title: Spore Spreading
desc: Spread beneficial spores through the lower gardens. The fungus feeds the hive.

steps.text:
The lower gardens are the newest expansion of Mount Pillar's food production — freshly excavated chambers that need to be seeded with the bioluminescent fungus that feeds the hive. Spore spreading is the first step: introducing fungal spores to prepared substrate so they can colonize and grow.

The spore keeper hands you a sealed canister — glass, pressurized, containing millions of spores suspended in nutrient mist. The canister is warm. The spores are alive and impatient.

"Three chambers. Each one gets a full canister, distributed evenly. Spray in sweeping arcs, cover every surface, walls and ceiling included. The spores will settle on the substrate and begin germination within hours."

She taps the canister. "Don't drop it. Don't open it outside the target chambers. Spores in the ventilation system would be... uncontrolled."

options.label: Check the substrate prep first — make sure conditions are right

steps.text:
The first chamber is ready — fresh substrate on the floor, walls moistened, temperature within optimal range. You unseal the canister. The nozzle releases a fine mist that catches the ambient light, shimmering with millions of microscopic spores.

You spray in arcs, as instructed. The mist settles on every surface — substrate, walls, ceiling. The spores are invisible once they land, absorbed into the material instantly. You cover the chamber in overlapping passes, ensuring no corner is missed.

The second chamber is identical. Spray, arc, cover. The canister's pressure drops as you use it — the spray becomes finer, requiring slower sweeps for even coverage.

The third chamber is furthest from the ventilation, which means it's warmer and more humid. The spores here will germinate fastest. You spray the final canister, watching the mist hang in the humid air longer before settling. The chamber glitters briefly — millions of spores catching the light from your lamp — then goes dark as they sink into the substrate.

Within three hours, the first signs appear. You return to check chamber one and find the faintest blue glow emerging from the substrate's surface — pinpoints of bioluminescence, thousands of them, like a constellation being born.

The spore keeper inspects and smiles. "Strong germination. These gardens will be producing food-grade fungus within two weeks." She marks the chambers as active. "The hive grows because the fungus grows. And the fungus grows because we tend it."

The blue glow strengthens as you leave. Three new gardens, waking up.

options.label: Watch the first blue glow emerge

steps.text:
The substrate needs to meet specific conditions — moisture content, temperature, pH, and nutrient density. If any parameter is off, the spores won't germinate, and a full canister of carefully cultivated spores is wasted.

You test each chamber with the field kit. Chamber one: moisture good, temperature good, pH slightly high. You add a dilute acid wash — reservoir water mixed with a mineral solution — and retest. pH corrected.

Chamber two: everything optimal. No adjustment needed.

Chamber three: nutrient density is low. The substrate here was mixed from a weaker batch — less Gloomuck mud, more filler stone. You supplement with concentrated nutrient paste from the supply kit, working it into the top layer of substrate.

The corrections take an hour. The spore keeper checks your work — testing each chamber independently, comparing readings to the target ranges.

"Good catches. Chamber one's pH would have inhibited germination by forty percent. Chamber three's low nutrients would have produced weak, pale fungus — technically edible but nutritionally poor." She seals the canisters for spraying. "The fungus is only as good as the substrate we give it. Garbage in, garbage out."

With the substrate corrected, spraying proceeds perfectly. The spores settle onto optimized material and begin germinating immediately. By evening, all three chambers show early blue glow — strong, even, covering the full substrate surface.

"This is how it should look," the spore keeper says. "Every spore landing on prepared ground. No wasted effort. The hive eats because we prepare."

options.label: Admire three perfect chambers of new growth
